statement.markdown: In a given grid, each cell can have one of three values: 0 representing an empty cell, 1 representing a fresh orange, or 2 representing a rotten orange. Every minute, any fresh orange that is adjacent (4-directionally) to a rotten orange becomes rotten. Return the minimum number of minutes until no cell has a fresh orange. If this is impossible, return -1.
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains two integers R and C, representing the number of rows and columns in the grid respectively.
The next R lines contain C integers each, representing the grid values.
statement.outputFormat: The minimum number of minutes until no cell has a fresh orange.

constraints.description: Number of rows in the grid
Number of columns in the grid

examples.explanation:
The grid is transformed as follows:
2 | 1 | 1
1 | 1 | 0
0 | 1 | 1
to
2 | 2 | 2
2 | 2 | 1
1 | 2 | 2
The grid cannot be transformed to have no fresh oranges.

hints.text: The problem involves a grid and requires simulating a process over time, where the state of one cell can change based on the state of its neighbors.
The process described is a classic example of a graph traversal problem, where the 'infection' or 'rottening' process spreads from rotten oranges to fresh ones.
A suitable approach involves using a queue data structure to keep track of the rotten oranges and then iteratively process them to infect adjacent fresh oranges, keeping track of the minutes passed.

editorial.title: Optimal Approach to Rotten Oranges Problem
editorial.intuition: The key observation is that the problem can be solved using a breadth-first search (BFS) algorithm, which is suitable for finding the shortest path (or in this case, the minimum time) to rot all fresh oranges.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve trying all possible sequences of rotting oranges, which would result in an extremely high time complexity, likely exponential in the number of oranges. This approach is impractical for large inputs.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach uses a BFS algorithm, which starts from the rotten oranges and explores the grid level by level, rotting adjacent fresh oranges at each level.
editorial.optimalSolution: The BFS approach has a time complexity of O(R*C) and a space complexity of O(R*C), making it much more efficient than the brute force approach.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the BFS approach can be proven by observing that it explores all possible paths to rot the oranges in a level-order manner, ensuring that the minimum time is found. The use of a queue to keep track of rotten oranges at each level ensures that no orange is rotted more than once.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the algorithm with a sample test case, such as a grid with a single rotten orange and a single fresh orange, helps to verify that the algorithm works correctly and produces the expected output.
commonMistakes: Using a depth-first search (DFS) algorithm, which may not find the minimum time to rot all oranges.
Not using a queue to keep track of rotten oranges at each level, leading to incorrect results.
Not checking for adjacent fresh oranges when rotting an orange, resulting in missed opportunities to rot oranges.
Not handling the case where there are no fresh oranges or no rotten oranges.
Not returning -1 when it is impossible to rot all oranges.
Not verifying the correctness of the algorithm with sample test cases.
